content.text: Agent skills are named, reusable instruction sets stored as Markdown files that Claude Code can execute on demand. They bridge the gap between ad-hoc prompting and fully automated hooks — a skill encodes a repeatable workflow that either a user or a running agent can invoke with a single command, passing runtime arguments that get substituted into the instructions before execution.

A skill is a Markdown file with a YAML frontmatter block and a body. The frontmatter declares metadata — a description shown in the skill list and an argument hint describing what runtime parameters the skill accepts. The body is the instruction prompt that Claude executes when the skill is invoked. Together, the two parts define a named, parameterised workflow:

---
description: Run the full test suite and summarise failures
argument-hint: [test-pattern]
---

Run the test suite for this project.
Arguments: $ARGUMENTS

1. Run `npm test $ARGUMENTS` and capture the output.
2. Identify failing tests and their error messages.
3. Group failures by root cause.
4. Suggest one fix per failure group, with code.
, Claude Code loads the file, replaces
, and begins a new turn with the resulting prompt injected as the user's message. From Claude's perspective, the skill body
the prompt — it has full access to tools, shell execution, and the project context, just as if the user had typed those instructions directly.
Skills can also be invoked programmatically from within a running agent turn using the
tool. This enables agents to compose workflows: an orchestrating agent can delegate a well-defined sub-task to a named skill without duplicating the skill's instructions in its own prompt.

Without skills, repeatable workflows exist only in memory. A developer who runs the same deploy sequence every week either types the instructions fresh each time, or pastes them from a note — both approaches are fragile, non-shareable, and drift over time. Skills codify that institutional knowledge into the repository itself, making it version-controlled, discoverable, and executable with one command.
Three specific problems skills solve better than alternatives:
A new team member who clones the repo automatically gets every team skill. Typing
works on day one without needing a runbook.
A multi-step orchestrating agent can invoke
as sub-tasks rather than re-embedding those instructions in its own prompt, keeping each layer thin and maintainable.
follows the same checklist, regardless of who runs it or when. Drift from the canonical process is impossible.
are available to every developer after a
variable makes a single skill file cover multiple use-cases without branching logic.
Skills are Markdown; editing them requires no compilation, no deployment, and no restart.
Agents that invoke skills via the
tool can be tested independently of the skill's implementation.
Claude Code surfaces available skills to the model in the system prompt, so the model knows which named workflows exist without scanning the filesystem.
A project skill shadows a user skill of the same name; a plugin skill can provide a baseline that projects override locally.
Skill bodies are plain Markdown — they can describe conditional steps in prose, but they cannot execute
at load time. Complex branching lives in Claude's reasoning, not in the skill file itself.
is a single substitution of everything the user typed after the command name. There is no named-parameter binding or argv parsing at the skill-file level.
A large skill body adds to the context window on every invocation. Very long skill prompts (>5,000 words) can crowd out working context, particularly in agentic loops with many tool calls.
Like documentation, skill files drift from reality if not maintained alongside the codebase they describe. A skill that references an old script path silently produces incorrect results.

Skills rely on Claude's reasoning to follow their instructions. Unlike hooks, which run unconditionally, a skill can be misinterpreted. Critical enforcement (blocking a tool call) still belongs in a hook.

The body after the front matter is free-form Markdown. It becomes the user-turn prompt when the skill fires. The single substitution variable
is replaced with everything the user typed after the command name. If the user provides no arguments,
is replaced with an empty string.
When a skill name is requested — either by a user typing
— Claude Code searches in the following order, returning the first match:
— skills bundled inside installed plugins. Highest precedence.
in the repository root. Versioned with the repo; shared by all team members.
in the home directory. Personal; apply to every project on the machine.
— skills shipped with Claude Code itself, such as
This means a project team can override any user or built-in skill for their repository by creating a file with the same name in
skill, however — plugins are always checked first.
token is a verbatim string substitution. Everything the user typed after
in the rendered prompt. The same applies when an agent invokes the skill via the

The diagram below shows the two invocation paths (user slash command and agent
tool call), the skill search order, and the execution model. Both paths converge on the same
substitution and prompt injection step before Claude runs.

content.text: The bottom row of the diagram highlights the scope hierarchy. A project skill with the same name as a user skill wins; a plugin skill wins over both. Built-in skills are the fallback when no custom definition exists.
Skills are the right tool when the workflow is:
Repeated more than two or three times — the authoring cost pays off quickly.
Parameterised — the same steps apply to different inputs (branch names, environment names, file paths).
Needs to be shared across a team or across agents in an orchestrated pipeline.
Complex enough that re-typing it each session introduces variation.
The goal is unconditional enforcement (blocking a tool call) — use a
The workflow needs to run automatically after every tool call — that belongs in a
The workflow is genuinely one-off — writing it in the prompt is faster than creating a file.
You need to expose a new capability as a tool that Claude can call — that belongs in an MCP server.
) becomes the slash command. Keep it lowercase with hyphens:

# .claude/commands/pr-review.md
---
description: Review a GitHub pull request for correctness, security, and style
argument-hint: <PR-number or branch-name>
---

Review the pull request: $ARGUMENTS

Focus on:
1. **Correctness** — logic errors, off-by-one errors, missing null checks.
2. **Security** — injection risks, auth bypasses, insecure defaults.
3. **Performance** — N+1 queries, unnecessary re-renders, blocking I/O in hot paths.
4. **Style** — naming, dead code, oversized functions.

For each issue found:
- Quote the relevant code.
- Explain what is wrong and why.
- Show a corrected version.

End with a verdict: LGTM / REQUEST CHANGES / NEEDS DISCUSSION.

# User invocation in the Claude Code REPL:
/pr-review 4321

# Or with a branch name:
/pr-review feature/auth-overhaul
and starts a turn with the rendered prompt injected as the user message.
Example 1 — Deploy workflow with environment gate

---
description: Build and deploy to the specified environment
argument-hint: <staging|production>
---

Deploy the application to: $ARGUMENTS

Argument must be "staging" or "production".
If omitted or invalid, stop and ask the user to specify.

Steps:
1. Run `npm run build` and verify it succeeds.
2. Run `npm test` — abort if any tests fail.
3. If deploying to production, show the git log since last deploy and ask for confirmation before continuing.
4. Run the deploy script: `./scripts/deploy.sh $ARGUMENTS`
5. Verify the deployment by hitting the health endpoint.
6. Report success or failure with the relevant log lines.

---
description: Create, review, or run a database migration
argument-hint: create <description> | run [--dry-run] | rollback <version>
---

Database migration task: $ARGUMENTS

Parse the subcommand from the arguments:

**create <description>**
  - Generate a migration file in `migrations/` using the naming convention YYYYMMDDHHMMSS_<description>.sql.
  - Scaffold the UP and DOWN blocks based on the description.
  - Show the file for review before writing.

**run [--dry-run]**
  - Run `db-migrate up` (or with `--dry-run` flag if specified).
  - Report which migrations were applied.

**rollback <version>**
  - Run `db-migrate down` to the specified version.
  - Warn the user about data loss before proceeding.
  - Require explicit confirmation.
When an orchestrating agent calls a skill via the
tool resolves the same search order as a user-typed command. The
A content publishing team uses Claude Code with three skills stored in their monorepo's
— finds the next unpopulated topic for the given subject, researches it, writes a structured document, and validates the output before committing.
— reads the generated document for the named topic, checks it against the editorial guidelines stored in
, and returns a structured verdict with line-level feedback.
— rebuilds the static site data, verifies all generated files exist, and pushes to the specified environment.
An automated pipeline agent orchestrates these three skills in sequence —
tool calls. Each skill is independently useful for human operators too, so the authoring effort is shared between automated and manual usage paths.
The team also maintains a personal

skill that each developer customises for their own morning workflow. Because user skills are below project skills in the search order, the team's canonical tools are never accidentally overridden by personal commands.
Overloading one skill with too many responsibilities
A skill file that handles ten different subcommands via
parsing becomes hard to reason about and difficult to test. Prefer one skill per well-defined task. If three variants are needed, write three files:

A skill body like "Deploy the app" produces highly variable results because Claude has no concrete steps to follow. Skill bodies should be as precise as a human runbook: numbered steps, explicit commands, acceptance criteria, and failure conditions. The test is: could a capable but project-unfamiliar engineer follow these instructions step-by-step and get the right outcome?
Forgetting $ARGUMENTS when no argument is expected
but the user doesn't type any, the substituted prompt will contain an empty string in a potentially confusing position. Either omit
from skills that take no arguments, or add explicit handling: "If
is empty, ask the user for the target environment before proceeding."
A skill that says "Never push to main directly" is advisory — Claude will try to follow it, but it is not guaranteed. If a policy must be enforced unconditionally, it belongs in a
hook that exits 2 when it detects a
Claude Code surfaces the description in the available-skills list that is injected into every session. A clear one-liner like
"Review a GitHub pull request for correctness, security, and style"
lets Claude choose the right skill when orchestrating without needing to be told explicitly.
Keep skill files short and action-oriented.
A skill body of more than ~1,000 words starts consuming significant context window space on every invocation. Move background context to
or separate reference docs that the skill instructs Claude to read.
Good skills specify what to do when something goes wrong: "If the build fails, show the last 30 lines of output and stop. Do not proceed to deploy."
Commit project skills to the repo.
should be part of the normal commit history. Review skill changes in PRs the same way code changes are reviewed.

Run the skill as a human user first with representative arguments. If the output is consistently wrong or requires significant cleanup, fix the skill body before incorporating it into an automated agent pipeline.
Even for agents that invoke skills programmatically, the
field signals the expected argument format, which reduces errors when Claude is composing arguments dynamically.

Skills have no runtime overhead at load time — they are resolved from the filesystem at invocation, not pre-compiled or cached in memory. The cost of a skill invocation is identical to the cost of the equivalent user-typed prompt. The only scalability concern is context window consumption.

In a long agentic pipeline where the orchestrator invokes five or six skills in sequence, the context window accumulates the skill bodies plus their outputs. If each skill body is 500 words and produces 300 words of output, six invocations add ~4,800 words to the session. Mitigation strategies:
for background") rather than embedding long context inside the skill body.
For long pipelines, a compaction step between skill invocations keeps the effective context manageable.
If two skills are independent (their outputs don't feed each other), running them as parallel sub-agents avoids accumulating both outputs in the main context window.
Claude Code ships a set of built-in skills that cover common developer workflows. These are always available and follow the same resolution order — a project file or plugin can override them by name:
Review a GitHub pull request across correctness, security, and style dimensions
Initialise a CLAUDE.md file by scanning the project structure
Launch and drive the project's app to confirm a change works in the real application
Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval
Complete a security review of pending changes on the current branch

Plugins package skills alongside hooks, custom agents, and MCP servers into a single installable unit. When a plugin is installed, its skills are added to the top of the search order, making them immediately available as slash commands in any session that has the plugin active. Skills bundled in plugins can be overridden by project-level files, giving teams flexibility to customise behaviour per repository.
Persistent project context, style guidelines, background
Extending Claude's tool palette with new capabilities

Skills are plain text files — they carry no special privileges at the OS level. The instructions they contain run inside the same Claude Code session with the same permissions as any user prompt. A skill that instructs Claude to
will attempt exactly that if Claude's permissions allow it. Review skill files in code review just as code is reviewed.
substitution is a raw string insertion into the prompt — it is not sanitised. A skill that incorporates
without clear guidance on its format could be misused if an unexpected value is passed. For skills invoked by untrusted agents in a multi-agent pipeline, validate or constrain
in the skill body itself: "If the argument is not one of staging, production, or canary, stop and report an error."

Skills from plugins installed via the marketplace are reviewed by the platform before listing, providing a trust signal that custom files written by individuals do not carry. For enterprise teams, enabling
through managed policy settings can restrict which skills are in scope, ensuring only approved, admin-distributed skills are available.
a saved clipboard entry that is also a playbook
. Without skills, every deployment is done by retyping or pasting the same set of instructions. With a
skill, the playbook is always up to date (it's version-controlled), always consistent (no copy-paste drift), and executable in one command (no copy-paste at all).
The analogy extends to the agent dimension: an orchestrating agent that calls

is equivalent to a team lead telling a junior developer "run through the standard review checklist for PR 4321" — except the checklist is guaranteed to be the authoritative one, and it will be followed exactly.

The key difference from CLAUDE.md is timing: CLAUDE.md context is always present, like background knowledge. Skills are invoked on demand, like looking up a procedure when you need it. Both shape Claude's behaviour, but skills keep the session context lean by only loading workflow instructions when they are actually needed.
. The Markdown body is the prompt.
is substituted with runtime arguments at invocation.
Search order is: plugin skills → project skills → user skills → built-in skills. First match wins; project files override user files.
Both humans and agents can invoke skills. Humans type
. Both paths resolve the same search order.
Skills guide; they do not enforce. For hard policy enforcement, use a
hook with exit code 2. Skills and hooks are complementary, not interchangeable.
Keep skill bodies concise. Move background context to
or referenced documents to avoid consuming large amounts of context window on every invocation.
Commit project skills to the repository. Review them in PRs. A skill file is as important as the code it orchestrates.
substitution is a raw string insert — treat it as untrusted input in skill bodies that accept arguments from automated pipelines.
